questions.question: What is the single most useful reframe for an agile transformation?
options: A culture change to be grown and sustained, not a project you finish
A project with a fixed go-live date and a percent-complete dashboard
A tooling rollout (Jira, boards, dashboards) that enables agility
A framework-selection decision between Scrum, Kanban, and SAFe
questions.explanation: A transformation is a culture you grow and sustain, not a project with an end date. The fixed-date/dashboard option is exactly the classic failure mode; tooling and framework choice are the easy 20%.

questions.question: What are the five ADKAR building blocks, in their correct order?
options: Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, Reinforcement
Awareness, Knowledge, Desire, Ability, Reinforcement
Desire, Awareness, Ability, Knowledge, Reinforcement
Awareness, Desire, Ability, Knowledge, Reinforcement
questions.explanation: ADKAR is Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, Reinforcement, and the order is strict. Knowledge (how to) precedes Ability (demonstrated practice), and Desire comes right after Awareness, which rules out the other orderings.

questions.question: In Lewin's three-phase model, which phase is the hardest, where you build readiness and challenge the status quo?
options: Unfreeze
Change (Move/Transition)
Refreeze
Force-field analysis
questions.explanation: Unfreeze is the hardest phase: it loosens the current equilibrium and creates the readiness Kotter calls urgency and ADKAR calls Awareness plus Desire. Change is the messy middle and Refreeze locks in the new normal; force-field analysis is a tool, not a phase.

questions.question: The Satir change curve's vital message is that performance gets worse before it gets better, and a change agent should announce this dip in advance.
questions.explanation: Performance dips into the chaos trough before integration lifts it above the old baseline. Pre-committing to the dip is precisely what stops the panic revert when the inevitable drop arrives.

questions.question: In Westrum's typology, which culture type does DORA/Accelerate research find statistically predictive of high software-delivery performance?
options: Generative (performance-oriented)
Bureaucratic (rule-oriented)
Pathological (power-oriented)
Hierarchical (authority-oriented)
questions.explanation: Accelerate found a generative culture (information actively shared, bad news welcomed, failure met with inquiry) predicts delivery performance. Bureaucratic and pathological are the lower-performing types, and 'hierarchical' is not one of Westrum's three categories.

questions.question: A manager fully understands why the org is going agile and could do the new behaviors, but privately does not want to lose their old control. Using ADKAR, where are they stuck and what is the right intervention?
options: Stuck at Desire; work the personal stakes and involve them in shaping the change
Stuck at Knowledge; send them to more agile training to close the gap
Stuck at Ability; give them hands-on coaching and time to practice it
Stuck at Reinforcement; add recognition and accountability metrics now
questions.explanation: They have Awareness ('understand why') but not Desire ('and I'm willing'), so the first missing block is Desire. More training (Knowledge) or coaching (Ability) is wasted on a Desire gap; you address it via personal stakes and co-creation.

questions.question: Why should you avoid using velocity as a cross-team productivity metric to compare teams and push them to 'increase velocity'?
options: It corrupts estimation and destroys psychological safety as teams inflate points and hide problems (Goodhart's Law)
Velocity cannot be measured reliably without a maturity score to first normalize it across the teams
Velocity only works in Kanban, so it is entirely meaningless during a Scrum-based transformation
It is essentially the same as lead time, so it double-counts whenever added to the DORA four keys
questions.explanation: Velocity is an internal planning tool; weaponizing it as a productivity score is Goodhart's Law in action, corrupting estimation and destroying safety as teams inflate points and stop telling the truth. The other options misstate what velocity is and how it relates to other metrics.

questions.question: Leadership mandated agile but middle managers are quietly blocking team autonomy. What is the most effective core of the fix?
options: Give managers a new, valued enabler role and bring them into the coalition as co-owners, with executive air cover
Remove the managers from their positions, since 'removing barriers' always means removing the people blocking it
Bypass the managers by having coaches work directly with the teams until the managers eventually comply
Tie the managers' bonuses to team velocity so they are financially motivated to deliver the change quickly
questions.explanation: The strongest fix recasts managers from task-assigners to enablers, involves them in designing the change, and secures executive backing so old controlling behavior is no longer rewarded. Removing them is a rare last resort, and bypassing or velocity-bonusing them makes the squeeze worse.

questions.question: The single most powerful antidote to resistance is involving the affected people in designing the change, because people support what they help build.
questions.explanation: Co-creation is the single most powerful antidote: a change designed with the affected teams meets far less resistance than one imposed on them.

questions.question: In Kotter's 8-step model, what is the 'classic killer' that causes an otherwise-progressing change to snap back?
options: Declaring victory too soon, before the change is anchored
Building a guiding coalition that is broad and cross-functional
Generating short-term wins within the first 6-18 months
Communicating the vision through many different channels
questions.explanation: Step 7 (sustain acceleration) warns that the classic killer is declaring victory too soon, when the change isn't anchored yet and reverts. Short-term wins and relentless multi-channel communication are recommended, not mistakes, and coalitions are meant to be cross-functional.

questions.question: Which of the following are the four DORA key metrics? (Select all that apply.)
options: Deployment frequency
Lead time for changes
Change-failure rate
Time to restore service (MTTR)
Team velocity
questions.explanation: The DORA four keys are deployment frequency, lead time for changes, change-failure rate, and time to restore service; the first two measure throughput and the last two stability. Velocity is explicitly rejected as a cross-team productivity metric, so it is not a DORA key.

questions.question: Which of the following are standard agile-transformation failure modes? (Select all that apply.)
options: Tooling and ceremony theater without behavior change
No leadership buy-in while leaders exempt themselves
Big-bang rollout with no slack to learn
Treating the transformation as a long-lived product with a backlog and vision
Building a guiding coalition with power and credibility
questions.explanation: Ceremony theater, no leadership buy-in, and a big-bang rollout with no slack are all listed failure modes. Treating the transformation as a product and building a credible coalition are the recommended practices, not failures.

questions.question: Why should you avoid tracking a transformation primarily with an 'agile maturity score' or 'percent of teams doing retros'?
options: They measure compliance with rituals, not value; they are trivially gamed and can rise while real performance is flat
They are far too expensive to compute when compared with simple outcome metrics like lead time and cycle time
They are proprietary to Prosci and therefore cannot legally be used without first purchasing a vendor license
They only apply to bottom-up grassroots transformations, never to top-down executive-mandated transformations
questions.explanation: Maturity scores measure ritual compliance rather than value, are easy to game, invite theater, and can climb while real performance stays flat (Goodhart's Law). Cost, licensing, and direction of the transformation are not the reasons.

questions.question: What is the fastest single diagnostic of where an organization sits on the Westrum spectrum?
options: What happens to the messenger who brings bad news
How many teams have adopted story points and velocity
Whether the org has a documented agile maturity model
How quickly the PMO disbands after the go-live date
questions.explanation: Watching one incident review reveals the culture: is bad news welcomed and learned from (generative), processed bureaucratically, or punished (pathological)? The other options measure ritual or structure, not how information and failure are handled.

questions.subtopic: Satir chaos stage in code-like scenario
questions.question: The pseudocode below models a transformation's monthly performance index (baseline 70). With month = 3, the condition is true. What is the senior change-agent's correct interpretation and advice?
options: It is the Chaos/dip stage; hold the line and increase support rather than revert, since Integration lifts performance above baseline next
The change has clearly failed already, so revert to the old way of working immediately to stop the ongoing loss of output
Performance is now permanently lower, so renegotiate the transformation's scope downward to match the reduced new ceiling
The dip proves the framework itself was wrong, so switch to a different framework and restart the whole rollout from scratch
questions.explanation: Index 2 (value 30) is the Satir Chaos trough: old methods fail and new ones aren't mastered yet, so performance is below baseline before climbing to 90 (a new, higher status quo). Reverting in the trough guarantees the org never reaches Integration, which is exactly the panic mistake to avoid.

questions.question: A change program runs a textbook Kotter campaign (urgency, coalition, vision, communication) yet a specific team still won't move. What is the right lens and why?
options: Switch to ADKAR to diagnose the individual block the team is stuck at; a strong org campaign still fails if individuals are stuck personally
Add a ninth Kotter step to the model, because eight steps were simply insufficient for this particular organization's situation
Abandon the Kotter model entirely, because running two different change models at the same time inevitably causes conflicts
Escalate to the executives to mandate compliance, because completing the campaign proves the remaining resistance is irrational
questions.explanation: Kotter mobilizes the system at the org level, but ADKAR diagnoses why a specific person or team won't move at the individual level. The two are complementary, not rivals; a brilliant org campaign can still fail if individuals are stuck at, say, Desire.

questions.question: In Lewin's force-field analysis, you make the most durable progress primarily by doing what?
options: Weakening the restraining forces (removing barriers) more than adding driving forces (pressure)
Maximizing the driving forces so they completely overwhelm any of the restraining forces
Keeping driving and restraining forces in careful balance to preserve the current equilibrium
Adding new restraining forces that make the old behavior harder to keep doing day to day
questions.explanation: Force-field analysis progresses mainly by weakening restrainers (removing barriers) rather than just piling on pressure; removing barriers usually beats adding pressure. Simply maximizing drivers without reducing restrainers, or keeping forces balanced, does not move the equilibrium.

questions.question: A high-performing agile team reverted six months after the external coaches left. Which change-theory diagnoses and prevention measures apply? (Select all that apply.)
options: It is Lewin's missing Refreeze, Kotter's 'declared victory too soon', and ADKAR's missing Reinforcement
Anchor the new way in hiring, onboarding, promotion criteria, reviews, and reward
Build internal capability (internal coaches, communities of practice, champions) so the change doesn't depend on a few people
Declare the transformation officially complete so the team stops over-investing in process
Replace inspect-and-adapt with a fixed annual change plan to add stability
questions.explanation: The reversion maps to a missing Refreeze, declaring victory too soon, and missing Reinforcement; prevention is anchoring in systems and building internal capability so it survives the coaches leaving. Declaring it complete is the very mistake that caused the snap-back, and inspect-and-adapt should continue, not be replaced.
